content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions month

## 1. Primer (2004) is a genius, low-budget sci-fi that demands your full attention.

**Score:** 414
**Body:** This is one of the most meticulously crafted time-travel movies ever made. It was shot on a tiny budget but feels incredibly smart and complex.  
You'll need to watch it more than once to piece everything together, and that's the best part of the experience.

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I watched this once, thought I was following it, then in a moment had no idea what was happening
  - Being an edgy teen i was like “i fully understand this movie”. Then i opened the graphic someone made of all the timelines and i was oblivious to 95% of them lol

One of my first big humblings in life 
  - it's pretty much impossible to follow. i will preface by saying i think primer is the best time travel movie ever made, but it's biggest strength is also it's biggest flaw; it's just too complex. as it should be, multiverse time travel theory is crazy. it also means the movie has great rewatch value, but you pretty much need to either watch an explanation after or take notes while watching and really scrutinize every word. the script is very tight and fast so every line of dialogue has purpose, and they don't ever give you a moment to breathe and digest, which just makes it even harder to follow. when you do get it though, it is so rewarding.
  - I've seen it more than once, but same thing
    - I've seen it like, 6 times, watched youtube videos explaining it, studied infographics. . . and yeah, same thing
      - My favorite review of this film said "I don't want to be spoon-fed, but at least unlock the kitchen door for me!"
2. One of the greatest science fiction kicked ever made. Doubly impressive that is was made for only $7000.

Primer was the only movie which I've ever rewatched immediately after watching it for the first time. Then I watched it three more times. After all that I think I understand about 80% of it.
3. The director was on his way to being a new Kubrick IMHO, but ended up stalking his girlfriend/starlet and has since disappeared. If I'm wrong does anyone have updates on what he's involved in?
  - Yeah it’s such a disappointment that he disappeared under dodgy circumstances. I love Upstream Color maybe just as much as Primer, and then I read his screenplay for ‘A Topiary’ I think it was called. It was incredible. That would have been of the charts if he could have gone on to make it. Although I think he described it as impossible to make anyway.
4. The dvd with the director commentary is key to understanding that movie. I love it 

## 2. Moon (2009) is a masterclass in minimalist sci-fi and a truly haunting character study.

**Score:** 266
**Body:** This is one of those films that proves you don't need a massive budget to tell a profound and gripping story. Sam Rockwell's performance is absolutely incredible—he carries the entire film on his shoulders.  
It's a quiet, intelligent, and deeply isolating movie that explores identity and sanity in a way that sticks with you long after it's over.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Directed by Duncan Jones, David Bowies son. I think it’s a fantastic movie!
  - Forgot about that! It's been ages since I've seen it. Gotta watch again.
2. Just watched this for the first time a few weeks ago. Sam Rockwell is phenomenal, and the movie is just perfect.
3. Fantastic score too. One of Clint Mansell's best.
4. Everything about the movie works. The concept is fantastic, blending a bunch of different types of plausible Sci-Fi. The script is tight and nearly flawless. Rockwell is pitch-perfect in both roles in every scene, and Spacey's delivery balances the flatness of an AI with just the right notes of emotion. The tension is perfectly balanced. The mystery is unraveled really well. The music is brilliant. The production design is amazing, especially for the budget. I was so glad to have seen it in theaters when it came out.
5. I feel like this movie has become a little bit forgotten since Interstellar came out, but it was quite popular before then. I'll have to rewatch it one of those days, I remember it being quite funny without being a comedy as well.
6. I saw this when it came out in theaters and was completely awed. Been a fan of Sam Rockwell ever since.
7. No need to use AI, I'd appreciate you telling us what you think in your own words
  - Why would someone use AI for three sentences?
8. Totally agree. I should do a re-watch soon.

## 3. Just watched the new "Frankenstein"

**Score:** 246
**Body:** Hey everyone,  
I just got out of the theater after watching the new "Frankenstein" movie, and I wanted to drop a quick recommendation for anyone on the fence.

Without spoiling anything, the film does a great job balancing the classic gothic vibe with more modern themes. It’s not just a retelling, it actually adds something fresh without disrespecting the original story. The atmosphere, the creature design, and the emotional weight all hit way harder than I expected.

If you’re into sci-fi horror, slow-burn tension, or thoughtful character-driven stories, this is absolutely worth seeing. It’s not your typical jumpscare fest; it’s more about mood, tragedy, and the consequences of playing god. Also, the performances were way better than I thought they'd be especially the actor playing the creature.

I’d say: go in with an open mind, don’t expect a straight-up action-horror flick, and you’ll probably enjoy it as much as I did.

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I saw it this weekend and thought it was beautiful.  I think anyone looking for horror will be disappointed. It does have an old atmospheric horror feel to it but no real scares that I can think of.
  - Del Toro has always been atmospheric horror.
  - I went in expecting jump scares and left thinking about life and monstrosity instead.
  - That’s what I expected more gothic mood less actual horror
2. Don’t forget the generational trauma and how it’s passed down from father to son, not once but twice! Loved that.
3. Del Toro has a way of making movies that feel artistic and fantastical while you watch them, but after the movie you continue to think about it and it begins to feel like like horror as the ideas sink in
4. It’s not about the consequences of playing god it’s about rising above and not becoming the monster people claim you to be. Like always the movie is about the monster not Frankenstein
5. I watched it last night on Netflix and liked it too. I bet it was great to watch it in the theatre.
6. I thought it was incredibly beautiful and am already on my second watch.

I was shocked by Jacob Elordi. Up until now I'd pretty much dismissed him as Hollywood's latest jock prettyboy (although I did think he was good in Saltburn) but holy shit am I eating my words. He was outstanding as the creature, he made me cry several times.

I'm not sure how I feel about the departures from the novel, but it was still a gorgeous film.

Now please please please we need a GDT Phantom of the Opera (the original Leroux, not the Lloyd Webber.) Guillermo has said he wants to do it, make it happen.
7. Excellent movie, brilliant visuals and settings. Definitely worth watching. Loved Oscar's performance. One thing I disliked was waltz's character arc before his death. Felt like it happened for the sake of happening. Waltz was definitely under utilised in this movie.

## 4. I need to get off my phone and watch movies again. Recommend me something, if it gets 10 upvotes, I’ll watch it and reply with what I thought of it.

**Score:** 242
**Body:** I feel like I haven’t watched anything good lately, most of the time I’m half-watching some trash movie or TV shows while scrolling on my phone. I need some strong recommendations. I’m open to pretty much anything.

Drop your best pick and I’ll give it a try, then come back with my feedback.

**Edit:**

Holy s\*\*t, I wasn’t expecting so many of you guys to give me movie suggestions. I am a man of my word though, and I’ll be watching every single movie that gets 10 upvotes or more. I’ll be watching at leas one movie a day until the list is completed, and I’ll reply to your comment before I watch the movie and again afterward with my thoughts on it.

The order in which I’ll be watching the movies will be decided by the number of upvotes, so I’ll go from the most upvoted comment to the least. So stay tuned and be patient, the list is now in the mind-blowing range of 100+ movies, but your turn will come, I promise.

**Movies watched & reviews:**  
\- Children of men  
\- Office Space  
\- Little Miss Sunshine  
\- Best in Show  
\- Blade Runner  
\- My Cousin Vinny  
\- The Princess Bride  
\- The Silence of the Lambs

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Tombstone 
2. Best In Show
3. Office Space 
4. Jackie Brown
5. Harold & Maude
6. Blade Runner.  Sci-fi isn’t usually my fav but this was good.

Goodfellas is always entertaining
7. My Cousin Vinny
8. Children of men
9. The Thing
10. The Silence of the Lambs

## 5. Movies that made you laugh so hard you cried

**Score:** 209
**Body:** I feel like I've exhausted the extent of my own repertoire/reserve of funny movies. I'd like to give something new a chance, so here I am, hoping you can help.

To give you an example of what I find funny, the new Naked Gun had me in tears at times (and I love the originals). The Office (US) is my favorite TV show of all time, so movies in that vein would work, too.

Edited to add: Y'all are amazing, thank you so much. I'm really enjoying the suggestions, especially your anecdotes around them. I'm getting some good ones! Keep it up!

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Airplane, surely
  - Don't call me Shirley!
2. The birdcage
  - It’s da choes. They make me fall down.
3. 40 yr old virgin
4. Tropic Thunder
5. Young Frankenstein.  Mr Mom.   Let it Ride.   
6. The Hangover....but ONLY if you haven't seen it. I was still laughing 2 days later.
7. None. But have always loved Rat Race
8. We are the Millers

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic month

## 1. What's the most cruel thing you've seen in a movie?

**Score:** 8985
**Body:** One scene in particular I could think of is this one from 1408 with John Cusack where his character "reunites" with his daughter for a short amount of time. Won't say anything else to those who haven't seen it!

EDIT: Goddamn, I never thought my post would get so big lol.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. "She begged for her life and for the life of the baby inside her. Ohhhh, he didn't know."

- John Doe in Se7en
  - CALIFORNIA STAY AWAY FROM HERE. JOHN DOE HAS THE UPPER HAND
2. The Green Mile, Del’s execution.
3. Shoe, roger rabbit
  - The Dip!
  - STOP. Lol I block this one out for a few months at a time but it always randomly comes creeping back & I feel a sense of panic because I can't save him. I'm 42.
4. Baseball Boy from Dr. Sleep.

Horrible
5. In The Grey when the guy falls out of the tree and his daughter is standing over him with her hair draping over his face and then it cuts to the reality of wolves eating his face.
  - For me it’s the dude in the river who dies like an inch below the surface. Brutal
6. The end of Funny Games where they nonchalantly tossed that woman in the lake to drown. Even though the entire movie is about pointless cruelty, that last act struck me as particularly cruel and disrespectful.

## 2. Actors who suffer from Christoph Waltz Syndrome?

**Score:** 8655
**Body:** **Christoph Waltz Syndrome: great actors who were in some great films so producers assume they’ll naturally elevate a mediocre film.**

In the aftermath of *Inglorious Basterds* & *Django Unchained,* Waltz started to get cast in projects like *Tarzan.* While he’s very charismatic and engaging, an actor can only do so much with weak material. Simply casting someone like Christoph Waltz isn’t enough to bump your film up a notch. I think the most glaring example is *Spectre,* where Waltz is utterly wasted on a flat, shallow character who does little to nothing over the course of his screentime. 

These aren’t actors who simply take bad roles, they’re actors who are victims of their own success, somehow expected to be their own writer and director. Actors who were obviously shoehorned in thanks to a note from producers who don’t understand how filmmaking or acting works. 

As a side note, I’m not saying Waltz’s career has flatlined. He’s recently appeared in *Only Murders in the Building* in a role that perfectly suits him. It’s just a shame that he’s a supporting player in a streaming show - most of us can remember when he was meant to be the next Anthony Hopkins. 

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Christopher Lee said "You can be in a terrible film, but you can't be terrible in it" 

The man was never out of work
  - I mean have you seen Howling 2: Your Sister is a Werewolf?  Every time he appears on screen he absolutely outshines everyone.  

Also Tim Curry, Christopher Walken, and Patrick Stewart belong in this discussion.  Tim would be working like crazy if he didn't have that stroke.
2. I can’t believe they fumbled the Waltz Blofeld so badly.  It was solely in the writing.  Making him related to Bond was so immeasurably stupid.
  - Absolutely beyond dumb. But honestly, after what happened with Kahn and Reboot Trek, I wasn't really surprised.
3. Willem Dafoe, without a doubt. The man is an incredible actor and virtuoso but I've seen him in some bad films where he is the best actor in it. There are some films even his great acting cannot save. I think Gary Oldman, Max Von Sydow, Paul Giamatti and Steve Buscemi also qualify for this.
  - Eggers is giving Dafoe the weird and interesting series of roles his talent deserves.
4. The idea that all actors should be trying to get only the best roles and reject all others, to only seek acclaim, is to forget that they are artists and people with personal interests. A lot of the best artists don't care if they please everyone, because that is impossible. And part of being human is making mistakes, taking a part with a director they don't get along with, choosing a film that sounds good on paper but is executed poorly or ruined in the editing room.  

This sub often wants to eschew popcorn flicks, Marvel movies, because they are too corporate, too focused on commercial success. Then turns right around and criticizes actors who misstep and work on a film that is less than perfect, or fumble a single role, because it hurts their commercial success. Completely hypocritical. This is a perfect example of why actors shouldn't give a damn about what people think.
5. Giancarlo Esposito

Aidan Gillen

Bryan Cranston
6. Waltz was great in Big Eyes
7. F. Murray Abraham, by his own admission. Watched an interview with him once where he said after winning the Oscar, he’d received a ton of offers for ok films for good money filming in exotic locales. 

Basically used the opportunity to get paid while traveling around the world after being a journeyman actor forever, assured by his agents no one would see them. But, apparently, a lot of these movies were picked up by cable TV and it hurt his film career, so he went back to theater and he’s had a nice resurgence again on TV in recent years.

## 3. Oscar wins that felt undeserved

**Score:** 7493
**Body:** This is is a post to vent my disbelief and frustration at some of the most baffling Oscar wins. Let me get this started; Eddie Redmayne for The Theory of Everything (2014), Rami Malek for Bohemian Rhapsody (2019)

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. What about the fact that Bohemian Rhapsody also won for best editing
2. Gweneth Paltrow
3. Entire Emilia Perez
  - this is funny but i completely forgot this movie ever existed until seeing your comment
4. Robert Shaw not even being nominated for Quint in Jaws just shows how shite these awards are
  - Also, Wes Studi as Magua in Last of the Mohicans. You don't even think he's acting, you just genuinely believe that is Magua. No Oscar nomination.
    - Wes Studi is a name i drop and im like , "trust me, you know who he is. " Heat, Deep Rising, fucking Mystery Men!. Dudes a bad ass
5. Oscar voters don't watch all of the movies.
6. Shakespeare in Love. 
It was charming and entertaining, but it was no Saving Private Ryan. Not by a long shot.  

Fucking Weinstein. 
  - Will never understand Gwyneth getting the nod over Cate.

## 4. Has There Ever Been an Objectively Great Actor Who Completely Fucked Their Career Than Mickey Rourke?

**Score:** 7115
**Body:** I struggle to think of another one who comes close.

Rourke came out swinging in the early 1980s’ with a series of edgy, offbeat roles in acclaimed films like Body Heat, Diner, and The Pope of Greenwich Village. Lots of actors have been called ‘the next Marlon Brando,’ but it was a more accurate for Rourke than it was for other: like Brando, he was handsome in a grittier, more unconventional way than most movie stars, and he often went for more daring roles than your traditional leading man.

He peaked in 1986-87, with 9 1/2 Weeks making him a box office draw and the double shot of Barfly and Angel Heart standing as his best performances yet.

This should’ve been the start of an incredible career, but it’s actually where the trouble starts: he develops an attitude, thinking he’s above playing the Hollywood game. He turns down meaty roles like Rain Man and Die Hard so he can do embarrassing passion projects like Johnny Handsome and Wild Orchid. He starts to become known as being difficult to work with.

In 1991, well past his marketable prime, he makes the decision to take up professional boxing, a truly baffling side project for an actor. He’s nowhere near as good a boxer as he is an actor, but he never got the hint and further alienated people from wanting to work for him. His films during this time - Harley Davidson and the Marlboro Man, White Sands, FTW - are forgettable and embarrassing compared to the films he made in his prime.

Then come the facial surgeries. I’d say mid-1995 is when the decline of his looks. He also starts taking steroids to the point where his voice loses the suave, smooth tone of his youth and drops about two whole octaves. By 1998 he is completely indiscernible as the guy from 9 1/2 Weeks. His looks severely limit the range of roles he can take and his attitude remains unchanged.

He spends most of the early 2000’s languishing in obscurity, doing made-for-tv and direct-to-video schlock. He gets a few meaty roles (Sin City, a great cameo with Nicholson in The Pledge) but nothing that changes the downward trajectory.

Then in 2008, he gets an unprecedented second chance with The Wrestler. Finally, a role that suited his current appearance and range. It’s a tour de force performance as good as any he turned out in his early career. It looked like the unthinkable had finally happened and Rourke was finally going to return to form.

Up to the Oscars, Rourke seemed to be making a genuine effort to rehabilitate his image and make good on the new opportunities afforded to him. He is charming, friendly, and even contrite in interviews. He even lays off the surgeries for a while and begins to vaguely look like his old self again. Even when he loses the Oscar to Penn, he does so diplomatically.

Then? He gets a big supporting role in Iron Man 2, and what happens? He has a not so great time making it and immediately falls back into the arrogant ‘above it all’ attitude, and resumes regularly fucking up his face with surgeries.

Nowadays he’s a complete joke and has completely fucked up his legacy. Future generations are only going to know him as a grotesque weirdo who made a few good films once. It’s a shame, because he could have been at the level of a DDL or Philip Seymour Hoffman. He could have been an undisputed great.

Anyone else you can think who royally fucked their career trajectory as spectacularly as he did? Closest I can think of is current day Johnny Depp.



### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Tom Sizemore (RIP) had an outstanding run of supporting roles in the 90's - Heat, Saving Private Ryan, Natural Born Killers, True Romance, Devil in a Blue Dress, Bringing Out the Dead, Strange Days, etc. Then meth, sex tapes, beating up Heidi Fleiss and other weird shit happened.
2. Chevy Chase is a huge comedic talent. Was the biggest thing in comedy but apparently an absolute nightmare to work with.
  - Chevy Chase and Keanu Reeves are perfect mirrors of each other. One was an undisputed brilliant comic actor who was so personally unlikeable he phased out of the A-list because nobody wanted to work with him.

Keanu Reeves was generally considered a bad actor and often made fun of for roles like Bram Stoker's Dracula but was such a good guy people in the business found roles for him suited to his strengths to the point that he's arguably way more popular today than he was in the 90s. They don't make a lot of Die Hard imitators like Speed anymore. He could have easily fell into the hole that swallowed Jean-Claude Van Damme and Steven Seagal. But just being a good guy made people want to work with him and now he's John Wick.
    - Keanu's skill set is limited but he puts in the work for what he can do. Martial arts and wire work training for The Matrix films, firearms training for the John Wick films. 
      - Dont forget totally excellent guitar playing and time travel
      - I was gonna say this. It’s not just that they make roles tailored to his strengths, but also that they can depend on him because he’s very dedicated to his work and the crew. I remember the BTS for Constantine and you could tell he was super invested into the role. I don’t think he’s ever had a major role where the production had problems. He’s even saved productions before.
        - After ‘The Matrix’ became a hit, he gave a bonus to the stunt people out of his own pocket because he credited them with the movie’s success.  He’s a REALLY nice guy, by all accounts. 

And he CAN act, though he’s definitely limited.  If you haven’t seen it, check out ‘The Gift’ by Sam Raimi, with Kate Blanchett (two more reasons to see it!).  He plays a sinister redneck, and he is EVIL!!!  Like, chilling.
3. You know, RDJ, but he had a comeback. When I saw Roark was cast as Whiplash, I thought Marvel was going to become a vehicle to rehabilitate actors that had become obscure.
  - Came here to post RDJ, people completely forget he was a tremendous fuckup. He went “home” one night and went to sleep in the bed of a neighbor’s kid. He couldn’t get a job anywhere and made less than almost everyone in Iron Man and was a pain to insure.
4. Looks like that Tim Robinson character in the old man costume

## 5. What are some hidden details that people tend to miss in films?

**Score:** 6812
**Body:** 
I recently read somewhere that in Shutter Island, >! there’s a guard standing right behind Teddy (Leo DiCaprio) and not behind Chuck (Mark Ruffalo), kinda giving us a glimpse of who Teddy really was all along…!< 

What are some interesting details or even clever foreshadowing that we tend to miss until particularly pointed out later during rewatches? Please use spoiler tags wherever necessary. 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. In Fight Club, when Tyler and the Narrator got on the bus, only the Narrator paid the bus fare.

But the audience ignored it because Tyler not paying the bus fare is in line with his character.

  
Edit:

Another one I just realized was that when Tyler meets the Narrator on the airplane, they both have the same briefcase.
  - And the guy that walks by bumps into the Narrator and Tyler, but only says "excuse me" to the Narrator.
2. In the same scene as your screenshot, Mrs Kearns appears to be drinking an invisible glass of water.  


Explained by Teddy blocking it out mentally due to his traumatic association with water.




My favourite little detail, though, is later when the guards are supposedly searching for Rachel Solando, they are in reality just hanging around, totally bored, throwing rocks into the water, etc.
3. A subtle, but meaningful detail in The Truman Show, "Vitamin D" is seen in his medicine, a nod to his artificial world without a real sun.
4. Leo is always asking for a light because he isn't allowed to have matches.
  - Ruffalo also has trouble handling his firearm when they arrive at the island and must disarm.  No DUSM would fumble around like he did.
5. If you’ve never read the book, spoiler alert: in Stephen King’s It, Pennywise has an arch enemy, another interdimensional being called The Turtle. It indirectly helps the loser’s club to defeat Pennywise. In the two movies, turtle imagery frequently appears in various scenes, a deliberate nod and wink. 
6. In the Sixth Sense, Bruce Willis’ character never moves any physical objects (at least in front of other people) and doesn’t interact with anyone outside of Haley Joel Osment’s character. Which was actually done pretty pretty well because there are multiple scenes where we as the audience make assumptions, ex. him showing up to the anniversary dinner and sitting in the already pulled our chair or when he is sitting across from the mother in the living room.
7. In The Departed, Everytime somebody dies there is an X in the scene.
8. Rosemary's Baby is full of foreshadowing. A lot of people miss that it is set in 1966 - though it has a 1968 release date. The baby is due in June which is 6/66.

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm month

## 1. Why did Darren Aronofsky's Caught Stealing come and go so quietly?

**Score:** 486
**Body:** I’m genuinely baffled by how Caught Stealing came and went without much noise. You’d think a Darren Aronofsky film starring Austin Butler and Zoë Kravitz would do better, especially a crime thriller this stylish and pulpy. But alas, that's the state of modern cinema. But audiences were also pretty lukewarm or unreceptive. Instead, it felt like a blip, even though it might be his most accessible and purely fun film in years. Obviously, blame it on Sony for refusing to give this one a festival bow and sticking it in the dog days of August. A director friend of mine who adores Aronofsky didn't even know he had a new film out this year. 

But the more I sat with it, the more it struck me that Caught Stealing isn’t just a mild detour for Aronofsky. It’s a film where his long-standing obsessions (sin, guilt, moral reckoning, divine punishment) collide with a real sense of play and fun. It’s Hitchcockian in precision and got a Coen-esque rhythm, which is a departure for him but still under his signature umbrella. 

What really stands out is how controlled it is beneath the chaos. Every twist and tonal shift feels deliberate, not indulgent. The humor never undercuts the tension. Aronofsky has often been accused of self-seriousness, but here, his control of tone is remarkable. 

What I also love is how tactile the film feels. 1998 New York isn’t treated like a nostalgia trap but a real, breathing city. The bars, the grime, the music, the fashion all pulse with life. Aronofsky doesn’t romanticize the era; he immerses you in it. 

It’s also one of the few recent films that really feels like it has movie stars again. Austin Butler channels that old-school charisma but grounds it with vulnerability. He’s magnetic without ever winking at the audience. Kravitz, meanwhile, gives my favorite performance of hers (although, hot take, I'm not a massive fan of hers). Together, they give the film an energy that reminds you why we go to the movies in the first place.

And yet, for all that, hardly anyone’s talking about it.

I would blame most of it on the marketing and the late-August release, but even those who caught (pun) it didn't really sing its praises that I believe are earned. Perhaps some viewers didn’t know what to make of a filmmaker known for Requiem for a Dream and The Whale delivering a crime comedy with a beating moral heart. And for cinephiles, one may summise that the heaviness of his recent films weighed on their decision not to seek this one out. 

I’d love to hear what others thought. Did Caught Stealing deserve more conversation? Do you see it as a minor work, or is it secretly one of his most confident films?

I wrote a full review, digging deeper into these questions and Aronofsky’s worldview in the film, if you want to check out my expanded thoughts, but put your thoughts down below!!  
[https://open.substack.com/pub/abhinavyerramreddy/p/caught-stealing-surprise-after-surprise?r=38m95e&utm\_campaign=post&utm\_medium=web&showWelcomeOnShare=false](https://open.substack.com/pub/abhinavyerramreddy/p/caught-stealing-surprise-after-surprise?r=38m95e&utm_campaign=post&utm_medium=web&showWelcomeOnShare=false)

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I don’t think Darren Aronofksy committed enough to the bit, for this to be a truly memorable experience. It tries to create a Big Lebowski/After Hours type movie about a regular dude getting swept up in an increasingly bizarre series of circumstances, but it is nowhere near as witty and wild as either of those films.

As it is, it’s a fine time to spend at the movies, but nothing truly special.
  - Though I agree that there's definitely elements of The Big Lebowski and After Hours in the movie, to me it felt more like he was going for a turn-of-the-century Guy Ritchie/Danny Boyle style film. Shallow Grave, Trainspotting, Lock Stock, Snatch. In either case, he didn't nail it and the end-product feels unlike those movies as well as very un-Aronofsky
  - This is the answer. A big ol’ cinematic “meh”. And when you’ve come from the heights of where Arronofsky’s been and that stacked cast it just feels like a waste.
2. Personally, I didn’t like the movie very much. But—aside from Pi—I prefer my Aronofsky big and boisterous. I’m the guy that likes Noah and The Fountain and Mother!

As far as the broader public, this kinda low budget, hardboiled noir is very successful on streaming. I don’t think it does as well in theaters anymore. The Nice Guys, which I love, was also a bomb, so I don’t think this response is new.
3. Probably bad marketing and a confusing trailer. The trailer made it look like there were a lot of comedic beats, and quirky characters. I have A-List so I took a chance on seeing it. I was not expecting how dark, and violent it was. The trailer made that initial mistaken confrontation in the hallway seem like it was played for laughs, yet in the movie he was beat so viciously he lost a kidney. The different factions looked like they were silly caricatures and were instead vicious killers. I enjoyed the movie, but man it was not what I expected at all.
  - Okay so yeah everyone is clocking my reaction to it. The marketing was totally off for this movie. I too wasn't expecting the body count to be so high and things to get so menacing. I mean poor mom, I feared for her too lmao
4. Going to be a very controversial take but, I think that basically a lot of people who are big cinema fans have sort of lost a lot of love for Aronofsky.

Not including *Postcards From Earth*, the last three Aronofsky films (*Noah*, *mother!* and *The Whale*) all were fairly mixed received by folks. *mother!* in particular was pretty widely mocked for it's unpleasantness and incredibly on-the-nose storytelling. Even those who liked *The Whale* mostly only cared about it because of Brendan Fraser rather than the story and script, which again, was considered to be fairly cliche and unsubtle.
5. I didn’t see it, but to me it looked like DA doing his best impression of a Guy Richie film. In the end it looked like trodden territory. Perhaps it was genius and had a bad trailer, but I was really disappointed by Mother so put me in the ‘blip’ column.
6. He’s entered his David O. Russell period. Movies with great casts, good scripts, and competent but bland execution, resulting in “fine” movies that become forgotten before you hit the parking lot.
7. There’s too much tonal dissonance between the quirkiness of its comedic side and the darkness of its crime-thriller side to sit well with mass audiences who wanted something more Lebowski-esque; and while it was a solid success with the more critical audience, it also had the misfortune to come out shortly before *One Battle After Another*, which just completely overshadowed it with that crowd. So that momentum carried away people who would still be talking about it, and they moved on and never looked back.

## 2. The Running Man: why would a unique director wilfully become generic?

**Score:** 351
**Body:** I watched The Running Man earlier in the week, and thought it was a perfectly functional, decent action-comedy. I also can’t remember the last time I’ve been more baffled by the existence of a film, to the point where it felt like a director was performing self-sabotage for us all to see. When I watch a bad film from the likes of Zack Snyder, I can still understand the intent behind the piece, and the fact that as a director, he is playing to his own sensibilities.

But The Running Man is the kind of disappointment that truly baffles me. Edgar Wright is a highly distinctive director, and unlike Wes Anderson, there has been no expression of fatigue from the audience, whatsoever. Despite this, Wright decided to forsake his own style. I appreciated Last Night in SoHo (even though it jumped the shark in the final act), because there, it still felt like Wright traded off his style to try something distinctive. But with The Running Man, it truly baffles me to no end why a director like Wright would, as a conscious decision, make a film that feels like a David Leitch B-side.

There are two moments in this film which show that, to no surprise, Wright is still capable of bringing his unique sensibilities to the film. One which involves bullets fired to the beat of a song, and another in which Glen Powell’s face moves in the same direction as his face on a TV behind him. Both moments were shown in the trailer, and are not indicative at all of the rest of the film. If Wright is still capable of creating such moments (I mean, why wouldn’t he be?) then why choose not to? Again, I would understand and support if he abandoned his traditional style to aspire to something (like what PTA did with One Battle After Another, or Spielberg with Schindler’s List), but this is not one of those instances. It is a talented director striving toward mediocrity.

EDIT: My [full review](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=WZulA36AdGU) of the movie!

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I think a big problem is that he made an unnecessary rod for his own back by sticking to the formula of the book too much.

The book is a depressing, dystopian tale of a desperate and sickly man who survives more often than not by being shuttled around, and hidden by others.

Wright wanted to make a zany action comedy about a tough, charismatic guy with anger issues, who fights his way out of trouble.

The problem is that he tried to map the latter concept onto the former, despite the fact it can't support it, and made little to no changes to accomodate it. It'd be like shooting The Shining as a comedy. But changing nothing fundamental about the story or structure. None of your beats are going to work.

Wright was simply a bad fit for the material. Even the 80s version (which has plenty of one-liners and satire) understands that the material doesn't work unless everyone involved treats the stakes as deathly serious.

But Wright (as much as I love him) seemingly can't make a film unless it's drenched in irony. Everyone is a cartoon character. And this works when the script and story are in perfect harmony with his vision. But when it's fighting tooth and nail against it, you get this. Neither fish nor fowl.

It's the equivalent of steak-flavoured ice cream. Two things that are great in isolation. But absolutely do not work together. Meaning the project was fundamentally misconceived from the start. It just couldn't pick a lane.

Wright was so restrained in trying to please/impress King while also trying hard to please his audience. The two sensibilities cancel each other out for the most part, leading to a strangely moot, grey experience.

## 4. Peter Watkins, the most prescient director you've never heard of, has died. His legacy, however, will outlive us all

**Score:** 311
**Body:** Peter Watkins died on October 30th, 2025. That his death came and went with little fanfare is a sad injustice, as Watkins is one of the most radical, ahead of his time filmmakers in history. But his talent is inversely proportional to his recognition: due to the stunning and difficult movies he made (in style and subject matter) and the virulent attacks from official parties and critics that he received, his work is often elusive and hard to find. But when found, it's a revelation. 

Watkins' bread and butter style of filmmaking is difficult to name, but the one I go with is pseudo-documentary, or docudrama. To put it simply, he makes documentaries on fake scenarios and presents them as though they're real, somewhat similar to found footage movies, or mockumentaries (although mockumentary implies that it belongs to the comedy genre, and Watkins's films are anything but comedic). His pseudo documentaries come complete with a running voiceover, talking head interviews, and people acknowledging the existence of the camera and it's operator. It's a compelling conceit, but it's the scenarios that Watkins crafts through them that makes his work so compelling. 

His most famous (but still critically underseen) movie is Punishment Park, released in 1971. It takes place in an "alternate" history USA where the McCaren act, which allows the president to jail those he believes will engage in insurrection, is in full effect. Those who are jailed are given a choice: serve out their sentence to it's completion, or participate in Punishment Park. The goal of the park is to cross a desert in under 3 days and reach an american flag, all the while running from police who will hunt you down. Fail to reach it in time, and you'll be forced back to prison, if you're not killed outright. Do it successfully, and you'll be set free... 

I've never seen a movie that's been able to boil my blood in one second then chill it to the bone in the very next. Even though it's fictional, it's difficult not to see the real world parallels of it's time and now. I first saw this in 2021, and the George Floyd murder and riots remained fresh in my mind. The police brutality on display in concert with the documentary format made the events hit really close to home, as did the increasing polarization and feeling of helplessness that permeated the entire film. On a technical level, it's also massively impressive how Watkins is able to craft an entire environment and world through little more than clever sound design and claustrophobic camera work. It's a vital piece of film that should be studied the world over, but when it came out, the film was violently attacked. Attacks from more conservative voices were expected, but critics also lambasted the film for presenting itself as a documentary, as though the documentary format was a sacred ground of truth reserved only for real world stories, and anything approximating it is blasphemy. These critics failed to realize that that was precisely the point: to make the viewers question what they saw and to encourage them to see all future documentaries in a critical light. 

That leads us to the other half of Watkins' style and goal of film: forcing the audience to reflect on what it is they see. His choice of political pseudo-documentaries was not arbitrary: he saw how video was/is becoming the principal form of both entertainment and information, and rather than add on the pile with the same thing that everyone else was making, he wanted to make movies that made the audience actually digest the media they consume. He took to calling the vast majority of movies "the monoform." He identified the monoform as, quote, "a rapid flow of changing images or scenes, constant camera movement, and dense layers of sound," end quote. He believed that these movies neutralized self-reflection and dulled the average viewer's senses so that their attention span would be unable to fully digest what it is they saw. What Watkins also took issue with is how it seemed that EVERY form of Mass Audio Visual Media took its cues from the monoform, so that whether you watched a movie, a documentary, a tv show, or a news program, the same techniques were being used. Watkins believed the end result of all of this was a destroying of a modern audience's media literacy and a stifling of projects that didn't conform to the monoform that could otherwise exist. 

Look at the modern day media landscape: TikTok reels and shorts from other websites give us cookie cutter videos that we consume and create with equal pleasure, promising us quick info or laughs and a chance to go viral. Debates range endlessly here and on Twitter over media literacy, and whether someone correctly consumed a piece of media or not. And, with only a few exceptions, the majority of Hollywood movies are made from the same template that consists of franchise potential, CGI spectacle and market research surveys. Watkins was right. He was right about all of it.

Now, we can argue about both his own methods and whether things are truly as bad as he believed they were. I DO think a movie can include fast cuts, roving camera movement, and layered sound design and still force the audience to reflect on what they saw. But the fact is that whether they follow the monoform or not, few movies have the goal of actually engaging the audience beyond surface level thrills. And the MAJOR problem isn't that the monoform exists, but that it comprises the overwhelming majority of mainstream movies. 

To put it simply, Watkins, in his craft, his subject matter, his theories, and his vision of the future, was staggeringly ahead of his time. His final film, La Commune, was released in the year 2000, after which he retired from filmmaking. And, ironically enough, it was in the 21st century where his ideas of mass media as a propaganda tool and independent documentaries vying for an unvarnished truth came to full fruition. In this respect he deserves to be put on the same pantheon as Orson Welles and Jean Luc Goddard. But unlike Welles and Goddard, he never did have that much recognition outside of his native Britain.

His radical output put him at odds not just with official institutions that provided his funding (hence his nomadic career), but with critics and professors, themselves the true gatekeepers of what movies they expose to the next generations and what movies they keep under lock and key. And the end result is Watkin's relative obscurity in comparison with other directors. On letterboxd, a site dedicated exclusively to film fans, his most popular movie is Punishment Park, which only has 32 thousand watches and 23 thousand ratings (it's worse on IDMB, where it only has 8 thousand ratings). Edvard Munch was once in the top 250 highest rated films, but every time the ratings threshold is updated, it's always on the chopping black due to it's lack of popularity. His availability on physical media has also been a factor, at least in the US. On DVD his movies aren't difficult to find, but on Blu-ray it's a goddamn minefield. Punishment Park USED to be readily available through Eureka, but it got discontinued recently, and now copies are quite elusive. The same is true of Edvard Munch, and many of his best movies seemingly never even got an official Blu-ray release, like La Commune. Of all things, Privilege got an official Region A Blu-ray, while Culloden and The War Game got a double feature, but only in Region B. 

I'm not saying that outside forces are exclusively to blame. Watkins work, by design was never made to be simple entertainment. It wasn't even meant to be exploitative and shocking. It was meant to rouse your emotions on a truly visceral level and engage your intellect long after you finished watching the movie. Because of this, even his shorter movies are deeply harrowing, and as his movies got longer and the subject matter more intense (his film Resan, a 14 hour movie about the nuclear age, is one of the longest in history), it becomes more difficult to sit down and get through his work. But when you do, it'll inevitably be some of the most eye-opening and enriching filmmaking that you'll ever get through.

Where to start with Peter Watkins? I think it's difficult to go wrong with either Punishment Park or The War Game, the latter about a hypothetical nuclear attack on England.. They're fairly short, for one thing, but they're pretty good distillments of what Watkins is about, and are, of course, genuinely great movies. People talk a lot about pairing Oppenheimer with other movies, but if you want to have some fun, watch Oppenheimer, then Fail-Safe, then The War Game. I think that'll be a pretty good trilogy. Culloden is also excellent. It does at times have a slight feeling of cheapness that can get in the way (a problem that's fully rectified with Punishment Park), but it's a minor complaint: the end result is a stunning debut film that proves you don't need a lot of money to make an impactful period piece: just a strong point of view and clever usage of your resources. Edvard Munch, his most personal movie, and La Commune, his final triumph, both jockey for the title of his magnum opus, but are extremely long and difficult to find, so I recommend working your way up to them. The same is true of Resan, or the Journey, which you almost have no choice but to watch it in segments, which Watkins actually recommends. Privilege is a bit of a strange outlier in his filmography, but it's ideas of pop stars as government puppets trying to regain their individuality remains potent. The Gladiators (or the Peace Game) is even more prescient, anticipating both Battle Royale and the Hunger Games with it's depictions of televised battles to the death, all done as a means to control the populace. The rest of his feature filmography break the rarity scale: these movies don't even have so much as Wikipedia articles, let alone official physical releases in english. Good news if you live in france, however. Of all places, Doriane Films has almost all of his work available on DVD. 

As you can see, Watkins is a difficult filmmaker to find his movies, he's a difficult filmmaker to get into, and he's a difficult filmmaker to fully comprehend. But nothing that was ever worth anything ever came easy. Peter Watkins is one of the greatest and most important filmmakers of the 20th century, and despite his passing, despite his obscurity, the arc of history has proven him right. Here's hoping that the arc of history gives him the proper recognition in death that he never got in life.

## 5. Bugonia is a perfect portrayal of the modern communication gap

**Score:** 310
**Body:** Of course, there aren’t many people who think corporate executives are literally aliens. But the feeling that something’s broken at the center of the world, and it’s powerful elites who are at fault…that’s more widespread than ever, on both sides of the political spectrum. That sort of despair at the current state of the world is one that both MAGA and progressives tap into (albeit for very different ends), and it’s not one that the establishment really seems able to handle. Trust in science, universities, and traditional news media is declining, and one thing that r/conservative and r/politics could very much agree on is that many of those in power are fundamentally out of touch with the people.

So how does that apply to Bugonia? It’s what I kept thinking about during the early scenes of Michelle trying to negotiate with Teddy. She always uses very polite, processed language that feels reminiscent of corporate press releases, and it’s just not at all capable of reaching Teddy, with his raw despair and anger at the state of the world. It’s not just that they have different opinions, but that she can’t even find a baseline with which to communicate and negotiate, and none of the discussions lead anywhere. I think you could definitely see this as a metaphor for, just to take one example, how the NYT’s audience shrinks every day while Joe Rogan’s grows. More and more people will only listen to those who can speak to their disillusionment in an emotional rather than dryly objective tone. 

Of course, a lot of this does get arguably muddled by the final twists, but I do think it all fits together in the end. >!If Teddy had been right about everything, I’d agree the film was validating him too much. But critically, the aliens weren’t doing anything to cause capitalist excess or climate change. That’s all on humanity, and it can’t be blamed on an overarching bogeyman of an international conspiracy. Teddy’s right that something’s rotten in the world—he’s just going with the most sensationalist answer instead of the correct one.!<

(This is my first time posting here, hopefully it was reasonably coherent)

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. A key scene in the movie was the "you mispronounced shibboleth" joke. Shibboleth was a hebrew word that was pronounced differently by different Israelite tribes, so a tribe could tell who was part of their group by the pronunciation. Michelle was part of a different tribe and they may use the same words but their words mean different things and they hold different values. It doesn't really matter if Michelle is a technocrat or alien, the movie is arguing that it is functionally the same either way. Her apology about Teddy's mother to calm Teddy down and her apology to Teddy in front of the press carry the same weight. Her biohacking and health technologies are essentially alien to the masses. A more lighthearted gag that ties into the alien/tech values was that all the Andromedans were racially diverse.
2. I don't think there really is a communication gap in this movie or in life, not to any meaningful extent. In real life, centrist liberals frequently claim when they lose elections (like Harris in 2024) that the issue was poor communication, rather than a rejection of their ideology by their voter base. I think the idea of a communication gap is largely a canard that gets those in power off the hook. 


Similarly, in the movie, there is no communication gap. She isn't making a genuine, good-faith attempt at communication (I would hope that's clear). She knows she's an alien, she knows he's correct, and she's trying to manipulate him. I wouldn't call one character using corpo-speak to try to gaslight someone a "communication gap." 


I think it's true there's an analogy between the characters in the movie, and real-world strategies used by those in power. It's like how sometimes those in power are willing to witness suffering, but not do anything about. They'll say "we hear you, we see you, you are valid." But won't upset the status quo.
  - This is a great analysis of what I think *Bugonia* is saying politically and a very logical rebuttal to the circular arguments OP has been repeating in this thread. 

Call them "centrist Dems" or "establishment Dems" or "technocrats,"  the people steering the Costa Concordia cruise ship that is the Democratic party are being paid a lot of money to avoid any evidence that their incremental policies are failing to win over working class voters. Instead they choose to view any failure on their part as a failure of "optics" and "communication style," causing them to waste their donors money on consultancy firms and marketing agencies to find the right way of speaking, the right font for their logo, some new hip way of presenting the exact same Bill Clinton Third Way policies instead of ever listening to the left wing of their party. 

Look at how the Democrats in power have responded to the success of Zohran Mamdani. He's about to become mayor of New York, mobilizing the youth vote to a crazy degree, after running a very modern campaign. He's produced ads starring himself where he talks to the camera and presents his policies in a charismatic way that normal people can respond to. Now you see establishment Dems trying to ape the exact style of his ads and graphics to much less success, and they're incapable of realizing that what makes Mamdani connect with people is the substance of what he's saying, how he clearly explains how he can make city buses and child care free for New Yorkers, how he can enact a rent freeze, how he can prioritize lowering the cost of living in a way that reaches those in the political middle through economic populism that improves their lives. The establishment Dems of today would think that the only reason FDR's Fireside Chats were successful was because of his relaxing voice or the jingle he used at the beginning. People kept voting FDR into office because he could explain how his policies would help people via clear practical language instead of focus-tested corporate word salad.
3. I feel like there's a lot of mental gymnastics required to read the film as about "the communication gap"...when it's quite plainly saying the ultra-wealthy and ultra-powerful see the rest of humanity as playthings that don't matter and that they can destroy. Your claim in the comments that the aliens aren't the 1% falls flat because, well, the 1% lady *is an alien*. You said they don't represent the 1% "because they don't do any damage" besides killing all of humanity...*but they did*. Her company turned the mother into a vegetable and ruined the boy's life. That kind of mundane damage is **real** damage. It's not like he's the only one that company has harmed.

If you think a bit deeper about it instead of looking for such literal metaphors, the message is extremely clear. "The people with all the power in this world hurt us in mundane ways *and* destroy us." The alien ruins lives for profit. The alien kills *everyone*. What do you think the 1% is doing in real life? The real life pursuit of unchecked power does all those things; climate change is our bubble pop, that's all (and just to hit that one home: we wouldn't have climate change without the pursuit of enormous, obscene wealth and power).
4. Late to this party but I saw the film yesterday.

>But critically, the aliens weren’t doing anything to cause capitalist excess or climate change. That’s all on humanity, and it can’t be blamed on an overarching bogeyman of an international conspiracy. Teddy’s right that something’s rotten in the world—he’s just going with the most sensationalist answer instead of the correct one.

My read is that >!there is dissonance between the Andromedons' self-image and their action. The Andromedons are _supposedly_ there to save humanity but we watch Michelle actively choose to be an overworking, cynical, performative executive whose company is almost certainly manufacturing the pesticides that are killing the bees. We spend the entire film going "well it makes sense that this guy is pissed off even if she's not an alien" because the film goes through such lengths to show us how despicable she and her company are. The people are just honeybees. I think about what Michelle says to Teddy as she's choking him "you can't beat me because you are a loser and I am a winner". Every single time Michelle is in a position of power over a human, she chooses cruelty.!<

To me, Teddy and Donnie primarily represent >!disenfranchised, working class, white male anger. Looking for some great narrative to give their life meaning. Picking up on genuine tensions but misdirecting them towards simplistic narratives of invasions, good and evil, and divinity, self isolating, and self mutilating. The sort of types that gravitate toward various internet grifters whether Alex Jones or Andrew Tate.!<

In contrast, Michelle >!and the Andromedons are sort of a caricatured self-righteous "liberal" elite who tell tell themselves sweet lies about how much good their doing while _(literally)_ **creating**, administering, and perpetuating the system of oppression they claim to be fighting. The Andromedons have zero actual empathy for any of the people in the movie. !<
  - This is a really good analysis, the best comment in the thread IMO. I would read things a little differently than you (more toward "enabling" than "creating") but that sort of ambiguity/gray area exists in real life as well.

6. This is a pretty centrist "why can't we all just be in the middle" reading of the film. I'll admit to being on the left side of the political spectrum to the degree that I identify with the big scary "S" word (Socialism), so to me Teddy stands as an example of someone disconnected from other working class people, who can't tie the pain he feels from losing his mother (in a situation that's directly reminiscent of Purdue Pharma's usage of Oxycontin to profit off of the opioid crisis) to a larger communal struggle. If regular people only see economic inequality worsening without a potential political solution, they're not gonna believe "establishment media" (which is increasingly pro-Trump anyway at the behest of their billionaire owners) and they're gonna blame somebody. Whether they blame immigrants, whether they blame aliens, or whether they go the Luigi Mangione route, it's all a big spiral of self-destruction. 

I was too young for the Obama "Hope & Change" era, but I was old enough to see the 2008 collapse really negatively impact the lives of the people I love. When Obama had a supermajority, he bailed out the banks, and he went with a Republican healthcare plan (Obamacare) instead of something universal resulting in not enough being done to convince working people that Democrats were actually going to make their lives better. Fast forward to the present day and you see Kamala Harris telling Jon Stewart in a recent interview that because Democrats don't control enough that "incrementalism is all that's left" and you can just see the light in Jon Stewart's eyes go out a little. 

The biggest laughs that *Bugonia* got in my screening were in Michelle's taped interview where she repeats the word "diversity" seven or eight times in different contexts. I remember the 2016 Democratic Primary, where Hillary Clinton would tell crowds "Breaking Up The Banks Won't Cure Racism" while trying to find some angle to attack Bernie. I just read an New Yorker article with Biden's former press secretary where she attempts to deflect the idea that maybe, just maybe, someone who looked **that bad** during the debate shouldn't have been the 2024 nominee AT ANY POINT if establishment Democrats actually gave a damn about defeating Trump. Such suggestions, according to her, were disrespectful to her "lived experience as an LGBTQ+ woman of color." 

I believe in ending racism, and I believe in equal rights for people regardless of how they identify in terms of gender or sexuality. I believe in taxing the rich to provide universal healthcare and better social programs to improve the lives of working people. Somewhere down the line, Democrats began to heavily emphasize the former to cover up how they were being paid a lot of money to avoid doing the latter. I don't think it's an either/or thing, and I think selling it as one makes everyone view Democrats as disingenuous. Now that Trump is president, you see centrist Dems like Gavin Newsom now attempting to walk back their support for trans people and "wokeness," essentially revealing that they don't actually believe in anything. 

Returning to *Bugonia,* I was surprised to see so many people take Michelle's speech explaining the motives of the Andromedans at face value. The aliens certainly weren't the sole cause of capitalist excess or climate change, but I think the apocalyptic ending is pretty damning for Michelle as well as Teddy. Here are some comments from /u/TheHouseofGryffindor and /u/WesternWobbegong in the /r/movies Discussion Thread I found insightful: 

> Obviously this is all up to interpretation, but I think [Michelle] being CEO of a major corporation is relevant in the overall message as well and not merely a necessity of the plot. Is recycling or reducing one’s carbon footprint important? Sure. But isn’t the majority of waste and environmental damage caused by corporations? Also yes. Those with the power to change things for the better often spend most of their efforts on the subject placing the blame elsewhere.

> [Teddy] spends the entire movie hypothesizing about the Andromedons to [Michelle's] denial, only to be proven correct time after time, be it her royal lineage or her hair being a way of signaling for help. I hesitate to state with absolute certainty that this time, she spoke without bias if not outright lies as she has up until now. While much is confirmed when she reaches her people, declaring the rulers as faultless but the subordinates as the problem feels out of place.

> When she pulls the plug on us, we see so much of the scope of humanity. Some of it mundane, be it a school or going back to [Teddy's] workplace, but a lot of it is people enjoying life. At a club, on a boat, having sex, preparing for a wedding, etc. This feels completely intentional. Despite the resolute triumph of the human spirit, she has one poor interaction with someone and writes off the entire race.

> I think the alien to human connection in this movie is very much like how the corpo 1% interact with the rest of humanity. They think they are above the wreckage created in the name of profit, but it ultimately gets everyone at the end. Very Nuclear Bomb. The bubble/ozone pop at the end is a great equalizer. Only the actual aliens who don't inhabit earth are okay (maybe that's why we got billionaires wanting to go to space so badly). At least nature's left to reclaim the earth though.
